title: 𐎀𐎖𐎅𐎚 — Aqht
titleEn: The Tale of Aqhat — Danel's vigil
draftNote: Draft in progress — the attested text follows accessible scholarly witnesses, cross-checked; a full collation against the standard edition is still pending, and line numbers in i 16–25 are approximate. One reading (abyn at / abynt) is a flagged crux.
source: Text: the attested readings of tablet RS 2.[004] (Louvre; = CTA 17 = KTU/CAT 1.17), column i, lines 1–33 — Danel's incubation vigil through the duties-of-the-son litany. Discovered at Ras Shamra (Ugarit) 1930–31; editio princeps Ch. Virolleaud, La légende phénicienne de Danel (Mission de Ras Shamra, 1936). No public-domain edition of Ugaritic can exist (the language was found in 1929), so Lectorium prints only the physically attested sign sequence — a fact no one owns — as reported by accessible witnesses (Gibson- and KTU³-based scholarly quotations, cross-checked); editorial restorations of the breaks are never printed as text — the notes report them. Damage brackets follow the witnesses; line numbers cite the CAT/KTU³ system, approximate (±1) in i 16–25 where accessible witnesses' lineations differ. Word dividers as printed by the witnesses. Transliteration: standard single-grapheme system (a i u for the three ʾaleph signs). Vocalized readings (the toggle) are comparative-Semitic reconstructions after the conventions of the standard handbooks (Huehnergard); actual vowels are unrecorded — the convention is applied consistently and is admittedly artificial. Font: Noto Sans Ugaritic (OFL).
about: The oldest story in this library with a named human hero: Danel, the righteous judge, has no son. For six days he feeds the gods and sleeps in the sanctuary; on the seventh, Baal pleads his case to El — and recites the famous catalogue of what a son is for, from tending his father's ancestor-stela to carrying him home drunk from the feast. Everything here is read from one scorched clay tablet dug up in 1930: the opening lines are broken, several words are cruxes no one can translate with certainty, and this app shows the damage rather than papering over it. What you are reading is exactly what survives.

sentences.l: […Rapiʾ]u — thereupon, the hero, […]

morph.gloss: break in the tablet — text lost

sentences.n: The poem's opening is gone: about ten lines are lost above this, and what survives of the first two lines is mostly broken away. From the formula's many repetitions editors restore: '[Then] [Danel, the man of Rapi]ʾu, thereupon the hero, [the man of the Harnamite]' — introducing our hero. Only the tail of rpi, the word aph 'thereupon', and ġzr 'the hero' physically survive. Danel (dnil) may be the ancient righteous sage Ezekiel 14 names beside Noah and Job. His two fixed epithets are imperfectly understood: mt rpi 'man of Rapiʾu' (a god? cf. the rpum, the deified dead) and mt hrnmy 'the Harnamite man' (his home town?).

sentences.l: girded(?) — the gods — he feeds; […] the sons of the Holy-One.

morph.gloss: crux word: 'girded' (clad in the ritual garment) or 'an uzr-offering' — meaning and vocalization uncertain
ʾilu — god; El, the high god (pl. ilm 'the gods')
enclitic -ma — emphasis; also the plural ending
break in the tablet — text lost
binu — son; bn qdš 'sons of the Holy One' = the gods
qudšu — holiness, sanctuary; the Holy One (divine epithet)

sentences.i: Girded, he gives the gods to eat, […] the sons of the Holy One.
sentences.n: uzr is a famous crux: either 'girded' — Danel wearing the ritual garment of his vigil — or an offering ('an uzr he feeds the gods'). The break is restored by editors as [uzr yšqy] 'girded, he gives drink' from the intact repetition below — your first taste of this poem's echoing, formulaic style. bn qdš 'sons of the Holy One' = the gods. The scene: childless Danel is hosting the gods, day after day, to win a son.

sentences.l: he-casts-off […] and-he-lies-down; he-casts-off […] and-passes-the-night.

morph.gloss: to cast off, throw down (yd 'he casts off')
break in the tablet — text lost
to lie down (yškb; cf. Hebrew šākab)
to cast off, throw down (yd 'he casts off')
break in the tablet — text lost
to pass the night (p yln 'and he passes the night'; cf. Hebrew lān)

sentences.i: He casts off […] and lies down, casts off […] and spends the night.
sentences.n: Editors restore [ṣth yʿl] 'his cloak, goes up' and [mizrth] 'his wrap' from the intact repetition at the end of the scene: 'he casts off his cloak, goes up and lies down; casts off his wrap and passes the night.' Danel is sleeping in the sanctuary on purpose — ritual incubation, awaiting an answer in a dream. mizrt (root ʾzr 'gird') is the same garment family as the crux-word uzr. Note p yln: p 'and so' + √lwn 'pass the night' (Hebrew lān).

sentences.l: behold, a day […]

morph.gloss: break in the tablet — text lost

sentences.i: Behold, a day […and a second…]
sentences.n: The lost words are restored as [w ṯn] 'and a second'. Here begins the count-up you will meet constantly in West Semitic poetry: a day and a second, a third and a fourth, a fifth and a sixth — then the climax on the seventh. The same seven-day ladder shapes Baal's palace-fire and the flood story's waiting doves.

sentences.l: […] the gods, Danel, […] feeds; girded(?), […the son]s of the Holy-One.

morph.gloss: break in the tablet — text lost
ʾilu — god; El, the high god (pl. ilm 'the gods')
enclitic -ma — emphasis; also the plural ending
Danel (Daniʾilu) — the righteous hero; mt rpi and mt hrnmy are his fixed epithets
break in the tablet — text lost
crux word: 'girded' (clad in the ritual garment) or 'an uzr-offering' — meaning and vocalization uncertain
break in the tablet — text lost
qudšu — holiness, sanctuary; the Holy One (divine epithet)

sentences.i: […] Danel [feeds] the gods, […] gives drink [to the son]s of the Holy One.
sentences.n: The second day, in the same formula, each line losing its opening to the break: '[and a second, girded,] the gods Danel, [girded, the gods] he feeds; girded, [gives drink to the son]s of the Holy One.' Only the tail -n of bn survives on the last line.

sentences.l: a-third, a-fourth day —
sentences.i: A third, a fourth day —

sentences.l: [girded(?), the go]ds, Danel — girded(?), [he fee]ds; girded(?), he-gives-drink-to the sons [of the Holy-One].

morph.gloss: break in the tablet — text lost
enclitic -ma — emphasis; also the plural ending
Danel (Daniʾilu) — the righteous hero; mt rpi and mt hrnmy are his fixed epithets
crux word: 'girded' (clad in the ritual garment) or 'an uzr-offering' — meaning and vocalization uncertain
break in the tablet — text lost
crux word: 'girded' (clad in the ritual garment) or 'an uzr-offering' — meaning and vocalization uncertain
to give drink (yšqy; cf. Hebrew hišqāh)
binu — son; bn qdš 'sons of the Holy One' = the gods
break in the tablet — text lost

sentences.i: [Girded,] Danel [feeds] the gods; girded he [fee]ds them, girded gives drink to the sons [of the Holy One].
sentences.n: The third day of the vigil, same formula, the breaks falling elsewhere — across the six days the repetitions let each damaged line be completed from its intact twins. This mutual restoration is exactly how editors work, and why the notes can report the lost words with confidence.

sentences.l: [a fi]fth, a-sixth day —
sentences.i: [A fi]fth, a sixth day —

sentences.l: girded(?), [the god]s, Danel — girded(?), the gods he-feeds; [girded(?),] he-gives-drink-to the sons of the Holy-One.

morph.gloss: crux word: 'girded' (clad in the ritual garment) or 'an uzr-offering' — meaning and vocalization uncertain
break in the tablet — text lost
Danel (Daniʾilu) — the righteous hero; mt rpi and mt hrnmy are his fixed epithets
crux word: 'girded' (clad in the ritual garment) or 'an uzr-offering' — meaning and vocalization uncertain
ʾilu — god; El, the high god (pl. ilm 'the gods')
enclitic -ma — emphasis; also the plural ending
break in the tablet — text lost
to give drink (yšqy; cf. Hebrew hišqāh)
binu — son; bn qdš 'sons of the Holy One' = the gods
qudšu — holiness, sanctuary; the Holy One (divine epithet)

sentences.i: Girded, Danel [feeds the god]s; girded he feeds the gods, [girded] gives drink to the sons of the Holy One.

sentences.l: he-casts-off his-cloak, [Dan]el — he-casts-off his-cloak, goes-up and-lies-down; [he-casts-off] his-wrap and-passes-the-night.

morph.gloss: to cast off, throw down (yd 'he casts off')
to cast off, throw down (yd 'he casts off')
to go up (yʿl; cf. Hebrew ʿālāh)
to lie down (yškb; cf. Hebrew šākab)
break in the tablet — text lost
girded wrap, ritual garment (root ʾzr 'to gird')
to pass the night (p yln 'and he passes the night'; cf. Hebrew lān)

sentences.i: Danel casts off his cloak — casts off his cloak, goes up and lies down; [casts off] his wrap and passes the night.
sentences.n: The sixth night, and now the formula stands intact — the very lines from which editors restored the broken openings above. yd ṣth 'he casts off his cloak': the root ydy 'throw down'; yʿl 'he goes up' (Hebrew ʿālāh) — up onto the sanctuary bed.

sentences.l: look — on-the-seventh of-days:

morph.gloss: then, look — marks the climactic seventh day
enclitic -ma — emphasis; also the plural ending

sentences.i: Then, on the seventh day,
sentences.n: mk marks the arrival of the climactic seventh day — the ladder of days (1–2, 3–4, 5–6) always resolves on the seventh. Singular drama after six identical days of silence.

sentences.l: [and-]he-draws-near, Baʿlu, in-his-compassion:

morph.gloss: to draw near (yqrb); as noun 'midst' (b qrb 'within')
Baal (Baʿlu), the storm-god; as common noun 'lord'
compassion, grace (root ḥnn; cf. Hebrew ḥānan)

sentences.i: Baal draws near in his compassion:
sentences.n: It is Baal — not El — who notices the suffering man. ḥnt is from ḥnn 'to be gracious' (Hebrew ḥānan): 'in his compassion', or 'at his (Danel's) plea'. Baal now speaks, describing Danel's misery to move El to act.

sentences.l: wretched are-you(?), [Da]nel, man of-Rapiʾu; sighing, the-hero, man of-the-Harnamite —

morph.gloss: Rapiʾu — divine name; cf. the rpum (Rephaim), the deified dead
Harnamite(?) — epithet of Danel, meaning uncertain (a place-name?)

sentences.i: 'Wretched you are, Danel, man of Rapiʾu — sighing, the hero, the Harnamite man —
sentences.n: An honest crux: our witness reads abyn at 'wretched are you' (two words), but some editions read a single word abynt 'wretchedness' — the difference is one ʾaleph sign, and the accessible witnesses disagree. Either way the sense is Danel's misery. abyn is Hebrew ʾebyôn 'poor, wretched'; anḫ 'sighing' — six days of unanswered vigil.

sentences.l: who — no son to-him like his-brothers, nor scion like his-kinsmen.

morph.gloss: there is not (ʾênu — the opposite of iṯ)
binu — son; bn qdš 'sons of the Holy One' = the gods
to, for; from; against; also emphatic/vocative la- 'verily, O'
šuršu — root; scion, offspring (cf. Hebrew šōreš)

sentences.i: who has no son as his brothers have, no scion like his kinsmen.
sentences.n: d in bn lh, literally 'who — there-is-not a son to him': possession in Ugaritic (as in Hebrew) is 'there is to X', not 'X has'. You'll see this constantly. šrš 'root' used of offspring — a scion; the pairing bn // šrš is classic parallelism: say it, then say it again in other words.

sentences.l: shall-there-not-be a-son to-him like his-brothers, a-scion like his-kinsmen?

morph.gloss: binu — son; bn qdš 'sons of the Holy One' = the gods
to, for; from; against; also emphatic/vocative la- 'verily, O'
šuršu — root; scion, offspring (cf. Hebrew šōreš)

sentences.i: Shall he not have a son like his brothers, a scion like his kinsmen?
sentences.n: Baal turns Danel's lack into a plea: bl iṯ 'is there not (to be)…?' — iṯ 'there is' (Hebrew yēš) is the positive twin of in in the previous line. Small print: our accessible witness actually prints it here; the existential is everywhere else iṯ, so we print iṯ and flag the discrepancy — pending collation against the standard edition.

sentences.l: girded(?) the gods he-has-fed, girded(?) given-drink-to the sons of-the-Holy-One —

morph.gloss: crux word: 'girded' (clad in the ritual garment) or 'an uzr-offering' — meaning and vocalization uncertain
enclitic -ma — emphasis; also the plural ending
ʾilu — god; El, the high god (pl. ilm 'the gods')
enclitic -ma — emphasis; also the plural ending
crux word: 'girded' (clad in the ritual garment) or 'an uzr-offering' — meaning and vocalization uncertain
enclitic -ma — emphasis; also the plural ending
to give drink (yšqy; cf. Hebrew hišqāh)
binu — son; bn qdš 'sons of the Holy One' = the gods
qudšu — holiness, sanctuary; the Holy One (divine epithet)

sentences.i: Girded he has fed the gods, girded given drink to the sons of the Holy One —
sentences.n: Baal reports Danel's six days of piety back to El, in the poem's own formula — with an enclitic -m added to uzr (uzrm), a little emphatic particle that changes nothing in translation. Formulae repeated with tiny variations: the heartbeat of this poetry.

sentences.l: wilt-thou-not-bless-him, O-Bull, El, my-father —

morph.gloss: to, for; from; against; also emphatic/vocative la- 'verily, O'
him (energic ending + 3 masc. sing. suffix on the verb)
to, for; from; against; also emphatic/vocative la- 'verily, O'
ʾilu — god; El, the high god (pl. ilm 'the gods')

sentences.i: Will you not bless him, O Bull El, my father,
sentences.n: The plea proper. l tbrknn: emphatic la- + 'you bless' + the energic ending -nn 'him' — a whole English clause in one Ugaritic word. ṯr il 'Bull El': El's standing epithet, strength and fatherhood in one image; Baal calls him aby 'my father'.

sentences.l: strengthen-him, O-Builder of-Creatures?

morph.gloss: him (energic ending + 3 masc. sing. suffix on the verb)
to, for; from; against; also emphatic/vocative la- 'verily, O'
builder, creator (bny bnwt 'Creator of Creatures', El's epithet)

sentences.i: strengthen him, O Creator of Creatures?
sentences.n: tmr from mrr — 'strengthen' or simply 'bless' (disputed; the root elsewhere means 'be strong/bitter'). bny bnwt 'Builder of Creatures' — El as creator; the second half of the parallel pair begun with ṯr il aby.

sentences.l: and-let-there-be his-son in-the-house, a-scion in-the-midst-of his-palace:

morph.gloss: to be, be established (ykn 'let there be'; cf. Hebrew kûn)
binu — son; bn qdš 'sons of the Holy One' = the gods
šuršu — root; scion, offspring (cf. Hebrew šōreš)
to draw near (yqrb); as noun 'midst' (b qrb 'within')
hêkalu — palace (cf. Hebrew hêkāl; ultimately Sumerian É.GAL 'great house' — see the Hammurabi texts)

sentences.i: Let there be a son in his house, a scion within his palace —
sentences.n: ykn (root kwn, Hebrew kûn): 'let there be, let him be established.' hkl 'palace' is the same word as Hebrew hêkāl — and ultimately Sumerian É.GAL 'great house', which you can see written in this library's Hammurabi texts. The blessing asked for; now comes the famous answer to 'what is a son for?'

sentences.l: one-setting-up the-stela of-his-father's-god; in-the-sanctuary the-ztr of-his-clan;

morph.gloss: to set up, erect (nṣb 'one who sets up'; cf. Hebrew maṣṣēbāh 'standing stone')
qudšu — holiness, sanctuary; the Holy One (divine epithet)

sentences.i: one to set up the stela of his father's god, in the sanctuary the emblem of his clan;
sentences.n: Here begins the catalogue of filial duties — recited four times in the poem, the fullest ancient answer to 'what is a son for?'. First duty: the ancestor cult. ilib = il + ab, 'the father's-god', the divine ancestor; skn is the standing stone (Hebrew maṣṣēbāh) that keeps his name. ztr is a genuine unknown — 'emblem'? even 'marjoram' has been proposed. No one knows; that is what a hapax looks like.

sentences.l: from-the-earth one-bringing-out his-smoke; from-the-dust the-guardian(?) of-his-place;

morph.gloss: to, for; from; against; also emphatic/vocative la- 'verily, O'
to go out; Š-stem mšṣu 'one who brings out' (cf. Hebrew hôṣîʾ)
smoke, incense — perhaps the dead father's 'spirit'
to, for; from; against; also emphatic/vocative la- 'verily, O'

sentences.i: to send his smoke up from the earth, to guard his place from the dust;
sentences.n: The most debated duty. qṭr is 'smoke, incense' — many take it as the dead father's very spirit, sent up from the earth by the son's rites; ḏmr is 'guardian/protector' or 'song' — both are defended. Read it as: the son keeps his dead father remembered, fed, and safe. Scholarly honesty: every word of this line is contested; the drift is not.

sentences.l: one-shutting the-jaws of-his-detractors; one-driving-out the-doer-of-ill against-him;

morph.gloss: jaws(?) — lḥt niṣh 'the jaws of his detractor'
detractor, reviler (root nʾṣ; cf. Hebrew niʾēṣ 'to spurn')
to drive out, expel (cf. Hebrew gēraš)

sentences.i: to shut the jaws of his detractors, to drive off whoever acts against him;
sentences.n: From cult to muscle: the son defends the father's name (shuts slanderers' jaws) and his person (expels troublemakers). d ʿšy lnh: relative d + 'the one acting' + 'against him' — the l of ln here is hostile, 'against'.

sentences.l: one-grasping his-hand in-drunkenness, one-carrying-him [when-]sated-with wine;

morph.gloss: to grasp, take hold (cf. Hebrew ʾāḥaz)

sentences.i: to take his hand when he is drunk, to carry him [when] he is full of wine;
sentences.n: The tenderest duty: the son walks his father home from the feast — takes his hand in škrn 'drunkenness' (Hebrew šēkār), carries him when sated (šbʿ 'be sated' — the same letters as 'seven') with yn 'wine' (Hebrew yayin). Ancient Near Eastern feasting was a religious obligation; so was getting father home afterward.

sentences.l: one-eating his-portion in-the-house-of Baʿlu, [and-]his-share in-the-house-of El;

morph.gloss: Baal (Baʿlu), the storm-god; as common noun 'lord'
ʾilu — god; El, the high god (pl. ilm 'the gods')

sentences.i: to eat his portion in the house of Baal, [and] his share in the house of El;
sentences.n: The son represents the father at the temple meals — eats the family's portion (ksm // mnt, another exact parallel pair) in the houses of Baal and of El, keeping the family's place in the cult alive after the father cannot.

sentences.l: one-plastering his-roof on-the-day-of [m]ud, one-washing his-gear on-the-day-of grime.
sentences.i: to plaster his roof on the day of mud, to wash his clothes on the day of grime.
sentences.n: And down to earth: the son patches the mud roof after the rains (ym ṯiṭ 'the day of mud' — Hebrew ṭîṭ) and does the laundry on 'the day of grime'. From stela to washing-line, the whole catalogue is one idea: a son is the father's continuation, in cult, in honor, in daily life. Here the pilot ends; the column continues with El's blessing — the next installment.
